title: Teacher AI Playbook
subtitle: Practical, role-aware guidance for subject teachers and subject leaders at Eduversal partner schools

audience._note: Subject leaders read EVERYTHING in this playbook (they still teach their own classes) PLUS the sections flagged subjectLeaderOnly:true. There is no separate subject-leader-only playbook — leadership-of-AI-integration responsibilities sit alongside classroom-teacher responsibilities, not above them.

_meta.purpose: What a subject teacher actually needs at 7:00 AM on Monday: a concrete, role-appropriate, Indonesia-aware guide to using AI in this week's classes without drowning in framework prose. Every block here is anchored to a citable reference — no invented 'best practices'.
_meta.referenceLayer: ../reference/ — verbatim Eduversal v1.0 + UNESCO + external sources. This playbook synthesises practical workflows from those sources; it does NOT replace them.
_meta.doNotFabricate: Every workflow / prompt / red-flag in this file carries a provenance object. Lint rule (Phase 1f) enforces non-empty provenance.

howToUseThisPlaybook.firstDay: Read 'orientation' (below), then 'redFlags' (the unconditional no-go list). That's enough to start using AI safely on Day 1.
howToUseThisPlaybook.firstWeek: Pick ONE 'quickWins' workflow and try it for one lesson. Don't try to overhaul everything.
howToUseThisPlaybook.firstMonth: Work through 'coreWorkflows.lessonPlanning' and 'coreWorkflows.differentiation' — these are the two highest-leverage areas per Anthropic's 2025 educator usage report.
howToUseThisPlaybook.everyWeek: Check 'weeklyTips' rotation (paired file: weekly-tips.json) for one fresh idea.
howToUseThisPlaybook.wheneverStuck: Use the 'decisionTrees' (paired file: decision-trees.json) for ambiguous situations.
howToUseThisPlaybook.ifYouAreASubjectLeader: Add the subjectLeaderOnly sections at the end of each major workflow. Your job is your own classes PLUS coaching your department through the same workflows.

orientation.what_this_is: AI is a TOOL, not a colleague and not a teacher. You remain professionally accountable for every decision in your classroom. AI accelerates the parts of your job that are pattern-shaped (drafting, summarising, brainstorming, restructuring); it does NOT replace the parts that require human judgement (assessing student work that counts, deciding what a child needs, talking to a parent, modelling Cambridge learner attributes).
what_changed_for_eduversal_in_2026: Cambridge International refreshed Primary + Lower Secondary Digital Literacy curricula in Feb 2026 to redefine 'digital maturity' for an AI-powered world. Coursework AI policy is now in the Exams Officers Guide Phase 3.

Permendikdasmen 13/2025 added Koding & AI as elective subjects (SD kls 5, SMP kls 7, SMA/SMK kls 10) — your students are now formally learning ABOUT AI. Your job is to model responsible USE of AI in your own subject teaching.

UU PDP No. 27/2022 became fully enforceable Oct 2024. Entering identifiable student information into public AI tools (free ChatGPT, free Claude, free Gemini) is now a regulated data-protection issue, not a personal preference.
the_two_questions_to_always_ask_first: Q1: Does this task require my professional judgement, or is it pattern-shaped drafting? — Pattern-shaped drafting (lesson plan skeletons, rubric drafts, differentiated practice problems, parent-comm drafts) is fair game. Judgement (grading summatives, deciding what a struggling child needs, assessing Cambridge coursework authenticity) is NOT.
Q2: Would I be comfortable if a parent / Cambridge moderator / school principal saw the prompt I'm about to type and the output I'm about to use? — If not, don't send the prompt.

anchors: reference:eduversal-v1-part1-teacher.foundation.domainC (teachers maintain professional role as instructional decision-makers)

quickWins.title: 30-minute lesson plan skeleton
quickWins.when: Use weekly when planning new lessons in your subject + grade
quickWins.workflow: Open your approved AI tool. Paste the lesson context (subject, grade, Cambridge syllabus unit, lesson duration, prior lesson, next lesson, student-population notes). Ask for: learning objective draft (2-3 options), starter activity (3 options), main activity scaffold, 3 formative-check questions, 2 differentiation moves (one stretch, one support), and an exit ticket. Spend the saved time on the parts only you can do: anticipating where your specific students will get stuck, and rehearsing your explanations.
redFlags: Don't paste student names or assessment data into a free-tier public AI tool. Use generic descriptors like 'a Year 8 class with 3 EAL students and 1 student with diagnosed dyslexia'.

anchors: manifest:externalAnchors.aiProviderEducatorGuidance.anthropic.educatorUseReport (lesson design is a top-3 educator use case)

quickWins.title: Differentiated practice in 3 versions
quickWins.when: Whenever you'd normally make ONE practice set and wish you had three
quickWins.workflow: Take your existing practice set. Paste it in. Ask for: an 'access' version (simpler numbers / shorter sentences / scaffolded), an 'on-level' version (refined wording, your existing target), and a 'stretch' version (extra rigour, transfer to a new context). Always review and edit. Differentiation is the #1 teacher AI use case in 2025-2026 because the time saving is enormous AND it directly improves your most diverse classes.
redFlags: Stretch ≠ harder numbers. Ask the AI to vary the COGNITIVE DEMAND, not just the difficulty of the arithmetic / vocabulary. Specify Bloom's level if you want clarity.

quickWins.title: Rubric first draft from a learning objective
quickWins.when: Whenever you set a new task that you'll mark formatively
quickWins.workflow: Paste the task and the learning objective. Ask for a 4-level rubric (Emerging / Developing / Secure / Exceeding — Cambridge band-friendly) with 3 criteria. Edit the descriptors. Test it on one piece of imagined exemplar work before using it for real. Rubric-anchored feedback prompts outperform open prompts because the rubric forces the AI to be specific.
redFlags: NEVER use an AI-generated rubric for SUMMATIVE assessment (anything that contributes to a reported grade) without department moderation. Formative use only at this stage.

quickWins.title: Parent communication draft (bilingual aware)
quickWins.when: Any non-trivial parent message — incident notes, progress concerns, positive recognition
quickWins.workflow: Tell the AI: the context (no student names — use 'a Year 7 student'), the tone you want (warm but professional / direct / celebratory), the language(s) you need (English / Bahasa Indonesia / both), and any cultural notes (Eduversal parents often expect more formal Bahasa than the LLM defaults to). Draft, then EDIT in your own voice. Never send AI output unedited — parents detect generic AI tone quickly and trust drops.
redFlags: Never include the student's name, class number, or any identifiable detail in the prompt. UU PDP No. 27/2022 — this is a real compliance issue, not just a preference.
Bahasa Indonesia from default LLMs tends to be slightly too formal in some registers and slightly too casual in others. Read aloud before sending.

anchors: manifest:externalAnchors.aiProviderEducatorGuidance.anthropic.educatorUseReport (parent communication is a top educator use case)

quickWins.title: Meeting / training notes summarisation
quickWins.when: After any PLC, training, or department meeting where you've taken rough notes
quickWins.workflow: Paste your raw notes. Ask for: a 3-bullet summary of decisions, a list of action items with owners (you'll add real names yourself), and a list of unresolved questions. Faster than typing it up properly, and forces clarity.
redFlags: If colleagues' performance was discussed in the meeting, don't paste those notes in. Personal-evaluation talk doesn't belong in a third-party AI tool.

lessonPlanning.title: Lesson planning with AI
lessonPlanning.philosophy: AI gives you a starting point, never the ending point. The lesson is yours. Spend the time the AI saved on the things only you can do — anticipating where your specific students will get stuck, rehearsing key explanations, and preparing the questions you'll ask.

steps.title: Set the context BEFORE asking for output
steps.do: Tell the AI: subject, year group, Cambridge syllabus reference (e.g. 'Cambridge IGCSE Biology 0610 Topic 2'), lesson duration, this lesson's position in the unit, prior lesson, next lesson, class profile (size, EAL count, SEN notes — anonymised).
steps.why: The 4-part mega-prompt structure (Role + Context + Task + Constraints) consistently outperforms one-shot requests across all 2025-2026 practitioner reports.

steps.title: Ask for a SKELETON, not a finished lesson
steps.do: Request: learning objective options, starter ideas, main-task scaffold, formative checks, differentiation moves, exit ticket. NOT a finished lesson plan ready to teach.
steps.why: Finished lessons feel generic because they ARE generic. Skeletons let your own judgement shape the lesson.

steps.title: Sharpen with follow-ups
steps.do: After the first output, ask: 'Where will Year 8 students typically struggle with this?' / 'Give me three common misconceptions and how to surface them.' / 'Suggest two ways a stronger student could extend the main task without doing more arithmetic.'
steps.why: Iterative prompting is what separates Practitioner-level AI use from Foundation-level. The first answer is rarely the best one.

steps.title: Edit ruthlessly. The lesson is yours.
steps.do: Cut anything you wouldn't say in your own voice. Replace generic examples with examples tied to your school context. Add the specific question you'll open with.
steps.why: Students can detect generic AI lesson tone. Your authority depends on the lesson sounding like YOU.

subjectSpecificNotes.math: AI is strong at: practice-problem generation, worked-example variations, real-world application scenarios. AI is weak at: novel proof strategies, deep conceptual scaffolding for fundamental misconceptions. Always verify problem solutions yourself before classroom use.
subjectSpecificNotes.english: AI is strong at: comprehension question generation, vocabulary lists, sentence-frame scaffolds, paragraph-model alternatives. AI is weak at: marking student writing for VOICE (it flattens to its own register), Cambridge-specific assessment-objective alignment. Use for drafting, not marking.
subjectSpecificNotes.science: AI is strong at: experiment write-up scaffolds, common-misconception lists, real-world examples linking to syllabus topics. AI is weak at: practical-skill assessment design (it doesn't see your equipment), region-specific examples (often defaults to Western/American contexts — explicitly ask for Indonesian or Southeast Asian examples).
subjectSpecificNotes.humanities: AI is strong at: discussion-question generation, perspective-prompts, primary-source paraphrasing. AI is weak at: balanced representation of contested topics (often shows a Western liberal frame by default), Indonesian historical or political content (verify dates, names, claims). Ask explicitly for 'multiple perspectives, including non-Western framings'.
subjectSpecificNotes.bahasa_indonesia: Per the 2025-2026 Bahasa Indonesia LLM benchmark, Gemini 3.x and Claude Opus 4.6 currently produce the most natural Bahasa. ChatGPT is correct but tends to be slightly more formal than typical classroom register. Read aloud before using with students.

subjectLeaderOnly.title: If you lead a subject department
additionalResponsibilities: Audit department members' AI-generated lesson plans periodically. NOT to police, but to spot common drift patterns (e.g. everyone defaulting to the same AI-generated starter activity).
Build a department prompt library shared in your school's Drive — your team's best-tested prompts for your subject. Better than each teacher reinventing the same prompt every week.
When walking through your department, ask one question in passing: 'What's an AI-assisted lesson move that worked this week?' Builds department culture without making AI feel surveilled.
Surface to your Academic Coordinator any pattern of AI overreliance (e.g. lesson plans that read identical week-to-week — a sign someone has stopped editing AI output).

toolsForThisWorkflow._note: Full tool list and metadata in manifest.json#toolRecommendations. Below: what works well for lesson planning specifically.
toolsForThisWorkflow.primary: Claude for Education or ChatGPT for Teachers — both strong for full lesson skeletons with iterative refinement. Pick whichever your school has approved.
toolsForThisWorkflow.differentTaskSameTool: When the lesson plan touches Bahasa Indonesia content (translations for EAL support, parent-facing summaries), Gemini for Education or Claude tend to handle Bahasa register better than ChatGPT.
toolsForThisWorkflow.specialisedAlternative: MagicSchool has dedicated 'Lesson Plan' and 'Unit Plan' tools that may be faster than starting from a general chat if your school approves it.

differentiation.title: Differentiation that wouldn't be possible by hand
differentiation.philosophy: Differentiation is where AI delivers the biggest classroom impact in 2026. Time was always the limiting factor — making three versions of a worksheet by hand isn't realistic for most subjects. AI removes that ceiling.

patterns.useFor: Practice problems, comprehension passages, writing prompts
patterns.how: Generate access / on-level / stretch versions. For access version, ALSO ask for embedded sentence starters or worked-example hints. For stretch, ask for an open extension question, not just harder numbers.

patterns.useFor: Any complex worded task in a non-language subject
patterns.how: Paste the task. Ask: 'Rephrase this for a Year 7 EAL student at intermediate English level. Keep the cognitive demand identical. Add a vocabulary glossary at the bottom for any term above CEFR B1.' Review the glossary — sometimes the AI over-glosses common words.

patterns.useFor: Topics where students reliably get one specific thing wrong
patterns.how: Ask: 'A common Year 9 misconception in this topic is X. Write three practice problems where this misconception would lead to a wrong answer, plus a feedback prompt that surfaces the misconception when a student gets it wrong.'

patterns.useFor: Word problems, scenarios, examples in any subject
patterns.how: Default AI output skews Western/American. Explicitly ask: 'Rewrite these examples using Indonesian contexts (currency in IDR, place names, foods, situations a student in Jakarta or Surabaya would recognise).' Audit the output — the AI sometimes invents plausible-sounding but wrong details.

antiPatterns: Don't generate ONE 'differentiated' version and use it without distinguishing who gets which version. Differentiation is about MATCHING, not just variety.
Don't ask for 'easier' content without specifying HOW. 'Easier' to an LLM often means shorter and simpler vocabulary, which is not the same as more accessible cognition.

anchors: reference:eduversal-v1-part1-teacher.practitioner.domainC (using AI to enable differentiation that would otherwise be impractical)

toolsForThisWorkflow.primary: MagicSchool AI — has dedicated tools for leveled text, scaffolded worksheets, and differentiated practice. Single-purpose tools tend to be faster than starting from a general chat for routine differentiation.
toolsForThisWorkflow.alternative: Diffit — best if you work from textbook passages or external source texts (paste a URL or text, get leveled versions back).
toolsForThisWorkflow.googleWorkspaceSchools: Brisk Teaching — Chrome extension that adjusts reading level inside Google Docs without leaving the document. Saves the context switch.
toolsForThisWorkflow.fallback: Claude for Education or ChatGPT for Teachers also work — slower than specialised tools but more flexible for unusual differentiation requests.

feedbackAndFormative.title: Feedback drafting and formative checks
feedbackAndFormative.philosophy: AI drafts feedback faster, but the JUDGEMENT — what this student needs to hear next — is yours. Never send AI-generated feedback to a student without editing it.

patterns.useFor: Class sets where you need consistent comment-bank style feedback
patterns.how: Paste your rubric. Ask: 'For each rubric level, generate a feedback template I can adapt. Include one strength I can name specifically + one next-step I can pose as a question + one practical action.'

Tell AI the learning objective + the most common misconception. Ask: 'Write 3 hinge questions where the wrong answer reveals the misconception. Each question should be answerable in <30 seconds.' Test the questions yourself first — sometimes the 'wrong' answer is actually defensible.

patterns.useFor: Closing routine across multiple lessons in a unit
patterns.how: Paste your unit's learning objectives. Ask for 10 exit-ticket questions, varying format (short answer, multiple-choice, drawing, ranking). You'll use one per lesson; bank the rest.

antiPatterns: Never use AI to mark anything that contributes to a reported grade. This is the single firmest red line in this playbook. AI does not see what your student understands; it sees what your student wrote.
Don't paste actual student names with their work into a free public AI tool. Paste anonymised samples ('student wrote: "…"') if you need feedback drafting on real work.

anchors: reference:eduversal-v1-part1-teacher.foundation.domainD (teachers understand AI outputs require professional judgment)

toolsForThisWorkflow.primary: MagicSchool — Rubric Generator and Text Feedback bundled with everything else you use it for. One login, less context switching.
toolsForThisWorkflow.googleWorkspaceSchools: Brisk Teaching — drop comments and highlights directly on student Google Docs. Faster than copy-paste workflows.
toolsForThisWorkflow.soloTeacherLowerPrivacyRisk: Eduaide.ai — teacher-only login (students never log in), so simpler privacy footprint. Strong feedback bot.
toolsForThisWorkflow.generalPurpose: Claude for Education or ChatGPT for Teachers — work well for one-off feedback template drafting if you don't use a specialised tool.
toolsForThisWorkflow.criticalWarning: All these tools draft. None of them MARK. The teacher marks. Anthropic's 2025 educator report found 48.9% of grading conversations fully delegated marking to AI — that crosses red line 2. Don't.

studentFacingAiUse.title: When students use AI in your class
studentFacingAiUse.philosophy: Your students are already using AI outside school. Cambridge International's Feb 2026 curriculum refresh + Permendikdasmen 13/2025 mean AI literacy is now a curriculum expectation. Your subject teaching either models responsible USE or implicitly endorses irresponsible use by ignoring it.

patterns.useFor: Any subject, any year group above Y6, ~one lesson per term
patterns.how: Generate an AI response to a typical task in your subject. Print it. Students annotate: what's correct, what's wrong, what's missing, what's superficial. Discussion. This single lesson does more for AI literacy than a dozen 'AI safety' assemblies because it shows students AI is critique-able, not authoritative.

patterns.pattern: Use AI as a study partner, not a homework-doer
patterns.useFor: Setting expectations early in the year
patterns.how: Demonstrate: a student who pastes a question and copies the answer = AI is doing the learning. A student who asks AI 'explain why I got this wrong' or 'quiz me on this topic' = the student is doing the learning. Frame the expectation as 'AI as a tutor, not a typist'.

patterns.useFor: Any work where AI use is permitted
patterns.how: Adopt one phrase: 'If you used AI for any part of this task, write one sentence at the end describing how.' Students mostly disclose honestly when the norm is clear and the disclosure is low-stakes. This matches Cambridge's coursework AI policy direction — declare, don't hide.

antiPatterns: Don't make AI a forbidden mystery. Forbidding it without engaging it pushes use underground.
Don't accept 'I asked ChatGPT' as a source. Students need to verify AI claims against textbooks, peer-reviewed sources, or you.
Don't run student-facing AI activities with a free-tier consumer AI tool that the school hasn't approved. Use only school-approved tools for live student use.

anchors: reference:eduversal-v1-part1-teacher.practitioner.domainC (teach students to use AI responsibly as part of subject instruction)

toolsForThisWorkflow._note: When students USE AI in your class, use tools designed for student safety, not general-public chatbots.
toolsForThisWorkflow.primary: SchoolAI — teacher dashboard shows every student's chat in real time. Built for supervised classroom AI use, not bolted on after.
toolsForThisWorkflow.mathSpecific: Khanmigo — tied to Khan Academy content, Socratic-style tutoring. Strong for maths classrooms. International pricing varies.
toolsForThisWorkflow.mostConservativeForYoungerYears: Curipod — students NEVER prompt AI directly; teacher controls all AI interaction. Best for Primary and Lower Secondary first introductions.
toolsForThisWorkflow.doNotUseForStudents: Free ChatGPT, free Claude, free Gemini, or any general-public tool — they have no classroom moderation and the data privacy posture for student use is wrong.
toolsForThisWorkflow.authoritativeRecommendation: OECD 2025 specifically endorses tools 'co-created with teachers' and tutors that 'ask questions instead of delivering answers' — the three above fit this pattern.

redFlags._note: Network-wide unconditional NO. No context shifts these. If a workflow or scenario lands you in red-flag territory, stop and re-route.

items.rule: NEVER enter identifiable student information (name, ID number, photo, parent contact, medical/SEN diagnosis, behaviour incident detail) into a public-tier AI tool.
items.why: UU PDP No. 27/2022 (Indonesia, fully enforceable since Oct 2024) treats this as regulated personal-data processing. The school carries the compliance burden, not you personally — but the school will trace the breach back to the prompt.
items.safeAlternative: Use anonymised descriptors ('a Year 8 student with diagnosed dyslexia') OR use only school-approved AI tools where data-handling is governed by the school's vendor contract.

items.rule: NEVER use AI to mark or grade anything that contributes to a reported grade or Cambridge-submitted work.
items.why: Cambridge coursework integrity + Eduversal academic-integrity policy + AI is not reliable at evaluating what a student UNDERSTANDS vs. what a student WROTE.
items.safeAlternative: AI can draft feedback comment templates that YOU then adapt for the actual student. AI can suggest rubric refinements that YOU then moderate with your department.

items.rule: NEVER use AI to author network-uniform assessments (chapter tests, EASE items, Cambridge mock papers).
items.why: Item-level quality, Cambridge syllabus alignment, and item-bias review are HQ subject-specialist responsibilities. AI-authored items leak factual errors, distractor flaws, and cultural biases that aren't caught without expert review. This is the formal assessment system — practice and gamification banks are different (see specialist-playbook.json).
items.safeAlternative: Practice questions for student self-study are fine. Formal assessment items are HQ-authored, even if HQ uses AI as a drafting aid internally.
items.provenance: reference:eduversal-v1-part1-teacher.foundation.domainD + Eduversal monorepo Common Mistake #33 (practice_questions never feeds formal grading)

items.rule: NEVER send AI-drafted communication to a parent without editing it into your own voice.
items.why: Parents detect generic AI tone within one or two messages, and trust erodes the moment they suspect they're reading boilerplate.
items.safeAlternative: Use AI to draft, then rewrite at least 30-40% of the message in your own phrasing. Add one specific detail only you would know.
items.provenance: reference:eduversal-v1-part1-teacher.foundation.domainB (refine and customise AI outputs rather than using them verbatim)

items.rule: NEVER use AI output as the basis for a high-stakes decision about a specific child (set placement, intervention plan, SEN referral, disciplinary action).
items.why: AI does not know your student. Even if you've described the student, the output is pattern-matching against generic profiles, not judgement about THIS child.
items.safeAlternative: AI can summarise documented evidence you've gathered. The decision is yours, in consultation with colleagues, the school's SEN/pastoral structures, and the family.
items.provenance: reference:eduversal-v1-part1-teacher.practitioner.domainD + UNESCO AI CFT Deepen-level human accountability

items.rule: NEVER use your personal-account AI for sustained school work involving student data, even anonymised.
items.why: Free-tier and personal-tier accounts typically default to using your conversations for model training. School-approved tiers (ChatGPT for Teachers, Claude for Education, Gemini for Education) have contractually-different data handling.
items.safeAlternative: If your school hasn't yet adopted a tier, raise it to your subject leader / academic coordinator. In the interim, restrict AI use to non-student-context tasks (your own training, generic resource ideation).

items.rule: NEVER ask AI to interpret a student's social, emotional, or pastoral state.
items.why: AI will produce confident-sounding diagnoses (anxiety, ADHD-like patterns, family-issue inferences) from minimal text. These are unsafe and outside your professional scope.
items.safeAlternative: Pastoral concerns go through your school's safeguarding / pastoral lead, not through a chat window.

shouldIUseAiForThisTask.summary: Quick mental flowchart before reaching for AI
branches: Is this a high-stakes decision about a named child? → NO AI. Use your colleagues and your school's processes.
Does this contribute to a reported grade or Cambridge coursework? → NO AI for marking. AI can draft a rubric for moderation, only.
Am I about to type identifiable student data? → STOP. Anonymise first, or use only a school-approved tier.
Is this pattern-shaped drafting (lesson skeleton, differentiation variation, rubric draft, comment template, summary)? → Yes, AI is well-suited.
Am I uncertain? → Use the decision-trees.json paired file, or ask your subject leader.

studentSubmittedSuspectedAiWork.summary: What to do when a student's submission looks AI-generated
branches: Do NOT accuse. AI detection tools are unreliable; false accusations damage trust and are usually wrong about non-native English writers.
Have a conversation: ask the student to walk you through their process. A student who wrote the work can explain choices; a student who didn't usually can't.
If the work is suspicious AND the conversation supports the suspicion, follow your school's academic integrity policy — do not improvise.
If you can't tell after talking, accept the work and adjust future task design (more in-class drafting, more process visibility).
studentSubmittedSuspectedAiWork.provenance: reference:eduversal-v1-part1-teacher.practitioner.domainD (evaluate AI-related academic integrity concerns, identifying potential misuse while avoiding false accusations)

parentAsksWhyWeUseAi.summary: Conversational response to a parent question about AI in class
keyPoints: Yes, we use AI in some teacher workflows — it accelerates lesson preparation and lets us differentiate more for individual students.
We do NOT use AI to grade work or make decisions about your child. Those judgements remain with the teacher.

Your child is learning to use AI responsibly as part of their education, because they will use it in further study and work — Cambridge International and Indonesia's Kurikulum 2025/2026 both recognise this.
We follow strict rules about not entering identifiable student data into public AI tools (UU PDP No. 27/2022).

progressionPath._note: Maps daily practice to the Eduversal v1.0 Foundation → Practitioner → Leader progression. Not a checklist — a self-orientation aid.
foundation_youAreHere: You use AI for one or two core workflows (lesson skeletons, differentiation, parent drafts).
You always review AI output before using it.
You never enter PII into public AI tools.
You can explain to a parent or student, in simple terms, what AI does and doesn't do.
practitioner_youAreReaching: You use AI across most pattern-shaped workflows in your week, with confidence.
Your prompts are iterative — you refine across multiple turns, not one-shot.
You teach students to critique AI output as part of subject lessons.
You spot subtle AI errors (cultural bias, dated information, fabricated citations) before students do.
leader_youAreShaping: You design AI-integrated curriculum and share it across your department / school / network.
You mentor colleagues through AI workflows, not just demo tools.
You contribute to school AI policy decisions with practitioner perspective.
Not all teachers need to reach this level — it represents specialist contribution, not the default expectation.
progressionPath.selfAssessmentRef: Annual self-assessment (Phase 2c) writes to Firestore ai_competency_self_assessments collection. Your line manager validates. The goal is professional growth, not performance judgement.

pairedDocuments.promptLibrary: prompt-library.json — kopyala-yapıştır prompts referenced by promptRef fields in this playbook
pairedDocuments.decisionTrees: decision-trees.json — full flowcharts for the decisionAids summaries above
pairedDocuments.classroomActivities: classroom-activities.json — lesson outlines for the studentFacingAiUse patterns
pairedDocuments.weeklyTips: weekly-tips.json — rotating fresh-idea card for hub dashboards
pairedDocuments.redFlagsRedlines: redFlagsAndRedlines.json — network-wide canonical anti-pattern list (this playbook's redFlags section excerpts from it)

subjectLeaderAddendum._purpose: Subject leaders read the entire playbook above as teachers. This addendum adds the leadership-of-AI-integration responsibilities specific to running a department.
weeklyResponsibilities: Walk through your department once per week and ask one passing question: 'What's an AI move that worked / didn't work this week?'
Add to the department prompt library: one new tested prompt per week, even if small.
Audit one department member's AI-generated lesson (with their consent) per fortnight — not to police, but to spot drift and offer coaching.
termlyResponsibilities: Run one department PLC slot per term on a specific AI workflow. Pick one from coreWorkflows above, demo it, have colleagues try it, discuss what didn't work.
Surface to your Academic Coordinator any department-level patterns: tools that work, tools that don't, where the school's approved-tools list falls short.
Review your department's contribution to the school's annual AI Maturity Assessment (Part 3 of the framework). Domain 4 (Teaching and Learning) reflects what your department does.
annualResponsibilities: Coach each department member through their AI self-assessment (Phase 2c). You are their line manager for this assessment.
Validate the agreed Foundation / Practitioner / Leader level for each team member. Be honest — performative inflation undermines the framework.
Identify 1-2 department members ready for Leader-level work (curriculum-sharing, mentoring, policy contribution) and propose them to your Academic Coordinator.
boundaries: You are NOT the school's AI policy authority — that's the school principal + academic coordinator.
You are NOT a disciplinary authority for AI misuse — escalate to the academic integrity / pastoral structure.
You ARE the department's first line of AI-practice quality, just as you are for lesson-plan quality and moderation.

openQuestions._note: Questions this playbook intentionally does not answer. Surfaced here so they're not lost.
items: What is the Eduversal network's approved-AI-tools list for the 2026-2027 academic year? — To be confirmed by Eduversal Academic Directorate. Until confirmed, this playbook references tool categories (Claude for Education, ChatGPT for Teachers, Gemini for Education) without endorsing a specific tier.
How does AI use interact with the existing Eduversal teacher appraisal weights (F1-F5 + F_LEAD)? — Phase 3 work; not yet wired into appraisal-framework-v2.json.
What is the school's voice on student-AI use in summative coursework? — Currently defers to Cambridge International's Exams Officers Guide Phase 3; school-level customisation pending.

Cultural-relevance bias in Western-trained LLMs for Indonesian classrooms — no 2025-2026 research found. Practitioner reports only. Playbook currently flags this as a known limitation in subject-specific notes.
